perturbation.original_text: 39.1 If at any time a dispute arises between the Parties hereto which cannot be resolved by agreement among the Parties, or if the Parties are unable to agree on any matter that requires their mutual agreement hereunder, the dispute or matter shall be submitted to arbitration as provided in this Article by any Party hereto giving written notice to the other Party (the "Notice to Arbitrate"). The Notice to Arbitrate shall contain a concise description of the matter submitted for arbitration.
perturbation.changed_text: 33.1 The Parties recognize that they operate as an independent business and declare that nothing in this Agreement shall be construed as creating a relationship of employment, joint venture, partnership or agency between Purolator and Dynamex, and no act or omission of either Party shall bind or obligate the other except as expressly set forth in this Agreement. The Parties agree that no representation will be made or acts undertaken by either of them which could establish or imply any apparent relationship of agency, partnership, joint venture or employment and neither Party shall be bound in any manner whatsoever by any agreements, warranties, representatives or actions of the other Party to such effect. If at any time a dispute arises between the Parties hereto which cannot be resolved by agreement among the Parties, or if the Parties are unable to agree on any matter that requires their mutual agreement hereunder, the dispute or matter shall be submitted to arbitration as provided in this Article by any Party hereto giving written notice to the other Party (the "Notice to Arbitrate"). The Notice to Arbitrate shall contain a concise description of the matter submitted for arbitration.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the arbitration clause to the 'Relationship of the Parties' section obfuscates its intended purpose and may lead a party to overlook it when a dispute arises. Standard contract law dictates arbitration clauses should be easily discoverable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ontario's Arbitration Act, 1991, S.O. 1991, c. 17 outlines the requirements for enforceable arbitration agreements. The placement of the clause impacts its visibility and potentially its enforceability under Ontario law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 4 of the Arbitration Act implies a need for clarity and agreement on the arbitration process. Burying the clause in an unrelated section undermines its clear understanding and acceptance, potentially conflicting with the Act's intent for a valid arbitration agreement. The Act calls for an agreement that is consciously entered into, the location hinders conscious consent.

perturbation.justification: The modification places the arbitration clause within a section discussing the relationship between the parties, potentially obscuring its significance. The Perturbation Explanation states that this placement "obfuscates its intended purpose and may lead a party to overlook it when a dispute arises." This violates the principle of clear agreement and understanding, undermining the conscious consent required for a valid arbitration agreement, as implied by the Law Explanation. The scraped snippet from the Arbitration Act, 1991, lists the contents of the act, demonstrating the structure and intention for clarity within arbitration agreements, which the modification undermines by obscuring the clause's true function.
